coredata.dc:title: Highly stable aqueous phase black phosphorus quantum dots with enhanced fluorescence property
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

Xu, Xiaoguang
Zhang, Xiaoshuang
Rong, Mayifei
Lu, Liying
Li, Yan
Dai, Wenhao
Du, Hongwu
Jiang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) are a kind of outstanding optical material due to the tunable band structure. However, their fluorescence property and stability are obviously weakened in the aqueous phase, which extremely restricts the development of BPQDs. Here, we propose a new method to prepare stable BPQDs in an aqueous solution directly with high absolute fluorescence quantum yield. Aqueous phase BPQDs are synthesized by liquid exfoliation and hydrothermal with NH2-polyethylene glycol (PEG)-NH2 as the modification agent to protect the BPQDs, which have high stability more than six months. In addition, NH2-PEG-NH2 is also a surface passivation agent to enhance the emission of BPQDs by forming P–N bonds, which is confirmed by an absolute fluorescent quantum yield of 11.5%. Moreover, BPQDs show excellent resistance to a strong acid environment and high ionic strengths except for Fe3+. Therefore, the BPQDs are a kind of highly selective and linear response fluorescence probe for Fe3+. Considering the good biocompatibility of BPQDs, they are employed as cell fluorescent label probes and show excellent fluorescence imaging contrast. Based on the unique structural stability and fluorescence performance in the aqueous phase, BPQDs are potential candidates for Fe3+ detection and optical bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black phosphorus
Quantum dots
Fluorescence
Iron detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and in vitro cell imaging

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the design of PEG@BPQDs and their applications on Fe3+ detection and cell imaging.
(a) TEM image of PEG@BPQDs. (b) HRTEM image of PEG@BPQDs. (c) AFM topographic image of PEG@BPQD. (d) Height profiles along the directions shown in the AFM image.
(a) XRD pattern of PEG@BPQDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of PEG@BPQDs. (c) P 2p spectrum of PEG@BPQDs. (d) N 1s spectrum of PEG@BPQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of PEG@BPQDs. The inset images show photographs of PEG@BPQDs in water under visible (left) and UV (right) light. (b) PL spectra of PEG@BPQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) PL spectra of BPQDs and PEG@BPQDs under the excitation of 320 nm. (d) Emission wavelength of L-PEG@BPQDs, PEG@BPQDs, and H-PEG@BPQDs under the excitation of 350 nm, 370 nm, and 400 nm. (e) PL intensity of L-PEG@BPQDs, PEG@BPQDs, and H-PEG@BPQDs under the excitation of 320 nm, 350 nm, 370 nm, and 400 nm. (f) PL spectra of PEG@BPQDs in ethanol or water under different excitation wavelengths.
Time-resolved fluorescence-decay curves of PEG@BPQDs (a) with and (b) without Fe3+ in water.

Stability of PEG@BPQDs. (a) The effect of storage time in the air on the PL intensity of PEG@BPQDs. (b) The effect of NaCl concentration on the PL intensity of PEG@BPQDs. (c) The effect of pH value on the PL intensity of PEG@BPQDs.

(a) The effect of different ions on the PL intensity of PEG@BPQDs. The concentration of each kind of ion is set to 500 μM. (b) Fluorescence responses of PEG@BPQDs toward different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 50, 70, 100, 120, 150, 170, 200, 250, 300, 350, and 400 μM). The inset shows the fluorescence intensities of PEG@BPQDs as a function of Fe3+ concentration, where F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of ions, respectively.

(a) The viability of the A549 cells after incubation with PEG@BPQDs for 24 h. (b) and (c) are confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells labeled with PEG@BPQDs in bright-field and excited by 405 nm. (d) Merged image of A549 cells.

head.ce:title: Highly stable aqueous phase black phosphorus quantum dots with enhanced fluorescence property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Chemistry and Biological Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) are a kind of outstanding optical material due to the tunable band structure. However, their fluorescence property and stability are obviously weakened in the aqueous phase, which extremely restricts the development of BPQDs. Here, we propose a new method to prepare stable BPQDs in an aqueous solution directly with high absolute fluorescence quantum yield. Aqueous phase BPQDs are synthesized by liquid exfoliation and hydrothermal with NH2-polyethylene glycol (PEG)-NH2 as the modification agent to protect the BPQDs, which have high stability more than six months. In addition, NH2-PEG-NH2 is also a surface passivation agent to enhance the emission of BPQDs by forming P–N bonds, which is confirmed by an absolute fluorescent quantum yield of 11.5%. Moreover, BPQDs show excellent resistance to a strong acid environment and high ionic strengths except for Fe3+. Therefore, the BPQDs are a kind of highly selective and linear response fluorescence probe for Fe3+. Considering the good biocompatibility of BPQDs, they are employed as cell fluorescent label probes and show excellent fluorescence imaging contrast. Based on the unique structural stability and fluorescence performance in the aqueous phase, BPQDs are potential candidates for Fe3+ detection and optical bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Black phosphorus (BP), as a metal-free two-dimensional (2D) layered semiconductor, has a typical puckered structure and stacked lattice configuration through weak van der Waals force [1–3]. Due to its unique optical and electrical properties stemming from its thickness and structural anisotropy of armchair and zigzag direction, BP has been widely used in field-effect transistors [4], photocatalysis [5], metal ion detection [6], and so on [7]. BP shows broad absorption across ultraviolet and infrared regions because of its layer-dependent band gap varying from 0.3 eV for bulk to 2.0 eV for a single layer [8,9]. Beside these, its low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and large specific surface area make it attractive in biomedical research [10].

Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs), namely zero-dimensional structural BP, are usually synthesized by sonication-assisted liquid exfoliation [4,11]. Compared with bulk or nanosheet of BP and some common biomaterials [12–15], BPQDs show excellent luminescence properties and have been widely used in bioimaging [16–18], cancer therapy [19], and sensing [20–22], which greatly expand the applications. BPQDs have been usually synthesized in organic liquids such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, and so on [16,23,24]. Since the organic phase BPQDs are toxic to organisms, BPQDs need to be transferred to aqueous phase before the biomedical applications, in which process the luminescence properties will be dramatically weakened. Moreover, BPQDs usually show poor stability in the atmosphere or aqueous phase, because BP becomes more sensitive to H2O and O2 as the thickness decreases. Superoxide ions (O2-) formed from O2 molecules accelerate the oxidation of BP to P x O y which is degraded by water into P
O
4
3 −
and P
O
3
3 −
[25]. So it is crucial to develop aqueous phase BPQDs with good photoluminescence (PL) properties and structural stability.

The low fluorescence quantum yield is another problem that hinders the functionalization of BPQDs. According to the previous studies, the size and surface state of BPQDs have a great influence on their PL properties [26]. Hence, the edge and surface passivation are powerful strategies to enhance the PL properties, which have also been widely demonstrated in other quantum dots. Many passivation agents have been applied to enhance the PL yield of BPQDs, such as metal ions, ethylenediamine, oleic acid, ethanol [16,27–29], etc. The lone pair electrons of phosphorus atoms are shared with passivation agents or metal atoms via covalent bonds, which results in both the strong PL and the structural stability of BPQDs. Inspired by this mechanism, we design a facile method to synthesize aqueous phase PEG@BPQDs using amino-modified polyethylene glycol (NH2-PEG-NH2) as the passivation agent to realize good stability and excellent PL performance. N atoms introduced by NH2-PEG-NH2 are attached to the surface of BP through P–N bonds, which are beneficial to improve the PL performance. Moreover, NH2-PEG-NH2 can not only strongly isolate the PEG@BPQDs from O2 to H2O but also improve biocompatibility and physiological stability.

In this work, PEG@BPQDs were synthesized directly in water by hydrothermal method, in which NH2-PEG-NH2 served as the surface passivation and modification agent. The design of the synthesis of PEG@BPQDs is shown in Scheme 1 , together with the applications of iron detection and cell imaging. As expected, the stability and PL quantum yield of aqueous phase PEG@BPQDs are significantly improved compared to previous reports on BPQDs which were synthesized in organic solution. Moreover, PEG@BPQDs are demonstrated to be high ionic selective probes and cell imaging agents.

BP powder was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Technol. Co. Ltd., China. NH2-PEG-NH2 (Mw = 2000) was purchased from Shanghai ToYongBio Technol. Co. Ltd., China. Ethanol, DMSO, and other chemicals with analytical grade were obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. All chemicals and materials meet the requirements of the experiments in this work and need no further purification.

10 mg BP powder was dispersed in 10 mL ultrapure water containing 50 mg NH2-PEG-NH2 and stirred for 10 min under the Ar atmosphere. Then, the mixture was sonicated at 300 W for 1 h. After exfoliation in water, the solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave chamber (50.0 mL) and then kept at 200 °C for 12 h. The sample was centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 10 min to remove the nanosheets, and the supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag. Finally, the nanoparticles were dried in vacuum overnight. The L-PEG@BPQDs and H-PEG@BPQDs were prepared in the same way except that the dosages of NH2-PEG-NH2 are 10 mg and 100 mg, respectively.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted using an Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The surface morphology of the PEG@BPQDs was measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by FT-IR spectroscopy on a Nicolet IS10 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterize the chemical valence by an ESCALCB250 Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to analyze the elemental content of PEG@BPQDs.

UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi) and PL spectra were measured with an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi). PL lifetimes and absolute quantum yield were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments PLS-1000 equipped with light-emitting diodes (excitation wavelength at 370 nm) at room temperature.

The detection of Fe3+ was carried out at room temperature in an aqueous solution of PEG@BPQDs under an excitation wavelength at 370 nm. To evaluate the influence of foreign substances, 1500 μL PEG@BPQDs were mixed with 500 μL 2 mM various metal ions, and then, the PL spectra of each mixed solution were measured with an F-4600 spectrophotometer under the same condition. The sensitivity of PEG@BPQDs towards Fe3+ was also studied. In a typical assay, a different quantity of 2 mM Fe3+ was added into 200 μL PEG@BPQDs (200 μg/mL) aqueous solution, and the PL spectra of the mixed solution were measured. The concentration region of Fe3+ was set from 400 μM to 10 μM.

The cytotoxicity of PEG@BPQDs was evaluated by the MTT assay. Human lung cancer A549 cells were seed in a 96-well plate with a density of 5 × 103 cells/well and cultured for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the culture medium was replaced with the 100 μL DMEM containing PEG@BPQDs of different concentrations (0–500 μg/mL) and incubated again for 24 h under the same conditions. After that, the medium was replaced with 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) and incubated for 4 h. All medium was removed and 150 μL DMSO was added to each well. The resulting complexes were shaken for about 10 min and the optical density was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader and the relative cell viability was calculated as Abssample/Abscontrol× 100%.

The A549 cells were cultured in a laser confocal petri dish for 24 h before bioimaging. Then the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of culture medium containing 100 μL PEG@BPQDs to culture for 4 h. Next, the cells were washed with PBS buffer three times. Finally, the images of cells were obtained by a confocal microscope.

To study the morphology and crystal structure of PEG@BPQDs, TEM and AFM were employed and the images are presented in Fig. 1 . PEG@BPQDs possess good dispersity with a spherical shape. The particle size distribution of BPQDs shows a narrow range from 1.6 nm to 3.2 nm with an average diameter of about 2.48 ± 0.5 nm, calculated by counting more than 100 nanoparticles (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Materials). The HRTEM image of PEG@BPQDs in Fig. 1(b) shows the highly-ordered crystalline structure of PEG@BPQDs and the lattice fringe is measured to be 0.21 nm, which can be ascribed to the (041) plane of BP. The topography of PEG@BPQDs is shown in Fig. 1(c), together with height profiles along the selected particles in Fig. 1(d). The thickness of PEG@BPQDs varies from 0.9 nm to 3.8 nm, which is larger than the diameter measured from TEM. This can be explained as the NH2-PEG-NH2 attached to the surface of PEG@BPQDs cannot be observed in the TEM image [6,30]. According to the thickness region, the PEG@BPQDs are composed of 1–7 phosphorus monolayers whose thickness is about 0.53 nm.

The crystal structure of PEG@BPQDs was further studied by XRD, and the pattern is presented in Fig. 2 (a). There are three sharp peaks at 2θ values of 17.3°, 34.5°, and 52.5°, corresponding to the (020), (040), and (060) planes of the standard card (JCPDS NO.47-1626), respectively. The peak at 20.5° is attributed to the (211) plane of SiO2 substrate (JCPDS NO.83-1339) [31]. The synthesized PEG@BPQDs take on a good crystalline structure. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to analyze the functional group on the surface of PEG@BPQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The absorption peak at 3423 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H and O–H vibrations. The peaks at 2876 cm−1 and 1459 cm−1 are related to the C–H stretching and the C–C bond [16]. P=O bonds at 1639 cm−1 and 1104 cm−1 caused by partial oxidation on the surface of BP are inevitable in PEG@BPQDs, though the oxidation of BP weakens the PL properties. [17]. Moreover, the peaks at 845 cm−1 and 889 cm−1 are ascribed to the formation of P–N bonds, which protect the PEG@BPQDs from further oxidation in the solution, indicating the successful modification and passivation on the surface of PEG@BPQDs [29].

To demonstrate the passivation effect of NH2-PEG-NH2, XPS was employed to study the chemical valence of PEG@BPQDs. The XPS survey results (Fig. S2) show binding peaks at 133.8 eV, 191.1 eV, and 399.4 eV, corresponding to P 2p, P 2s, and N 1s, respectively. The high-resolution P 2p spectrum is presented in Fig. 2(c). Two peaks are located at 130.8 eV and 133.9 eV, corresponding to the elementary substance and oxidized state of phosphorus. The peak at 133.9 eV can be separated into three binding energies of the P–N band at 134.6 eV and the P–O bands at 133.1 eV and 133.8 eV [32]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of PEG@BPQDs is presented in Fig. 2(d). The spectrum is fitted by three peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.6 eV, and 401.3 eV that correspond to the N–P, C–N, and N–H bonds, respectively [33]. The XPS and FT-IR results indicate that the NH2-PEG-NH2 is connected with BPQDs successfully and P–N bonds are formed, which proves the surface passivation effect of NH2-PEG-NH2. Accordingly, the PEG-BPQDs have a core-shell structure of phosphorus particle coated by NH2-PEG-NH2.

As one of the significant features of quantum dots, the optical properties of PEG@BPQDs are evaluated by UV-vis absorption. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the absorbance of PEG@BPQDs can cover the ultraviolet and the major visible light region from 200 nm to 800 nm. The PEG@BPQDs have a sharp absorbance peak at 296 nm, which is similar to that of the BPQDs obtained in NMP [4].

The PL behaviors of PEG@BPQDs dispersed in water and organic solvent were investigated to further reveal their optical properties. As shown in the insert of Fig. 3(a), the PEG@BPQDs aqueous solution is yellow-brown under daylight and emits intense blue PL excited by UV light. Like most reported BPQDs prepared by liquid ultrasonication and solvothermal in NMP, the PEG@BPQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the emission wavelength has an obvious red shift from 408 to 480 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 400 nm. The fluorescent intensity of PEG@BPQDs achieves the maximum at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. For comparison, untreated BPQDs were synthesized under the same experimental conditions without adding NH2-PEG-NH2, and the PL behavior was studied and shown in Fig. S3. The emission of untreated BPQDs is strongly affected by the excitation wavelength, consistent with that of PEG@BPQDs. However, the optimum excitation wavelength of untreated BPQDs shifts to 315 nm. As depicted in Fig. 3(c), the PL intensity of untreated BPQDs decreases significantly compared with that of the PEG@BPQDs with the same concentration. According to the previous report [34], the shift of peaks might be caused by the formation of P–N bonds on the surface of PEG@BPQDs. The lone pair electrons of N atoms can be easily transferred to the LOMO level in the sp3-orbit of the internal phosphorus nucleus, leading to a decrease of the band gap and the consequent redshift.

It has been reported that the PL properties of quantum dots are sensitive to the amount of surface passivation agent. To further investigate the quantitative effect of NH2-PEG-NH2, PL behaviors of PEG@BPQDs with different amounts of surface passivation agent were measured as presented in Figs. S4 and S5. Both L-PEG@BPQDs and H-PEG@BPQDs show an excitation-dependent fluorescence trend. Beside these, the emission wavelength in Fig. 3(d) shows a redshift with the increasing amount of NH2-PEG-NH2 under the same excitation light. It could be inferred that as the nitrogen content increases, more lone pair electrons could be donated to the LOMO level of the internal phosphorus nucleus, resulting in a further decrease of the band gap and a redshift in emission peaks [35]. Moreover, the maximum PL intensities of L-PEG@BPQDs and H-PEG@BPQDs appear at the excitation wavelength of 320 and 400 nm. It is noteworthy that both L-PEG@BPQDs and H-PEG@BPQDs show reduced PL intensity compared to that of PEG@BPQDs at the whole excitation wavelength region, as shown in Fig. 3(e). The reduced PL intensity of L-PEG@BPQDs can be ascribed to the insufficient passivation effect at a low amount of NH2-PEG-NH2. However, under a high passivation agent concentration condition, the excess NH2-PEG-NH2 molecules invade the remainder holes trapped on the surface of BPQDs, which weakens the emission property of the quantum dots [36].

Strong UV/blue light emission from PEG@BPQDs can still maintain when PEG@BPQDs were transferred to organic solutions. The emission curves of PEG@BPQDs in ethanol are presented in Fig. 3(f), which shows similar excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior to that in water. The PL emission peaks of PEG@BPQDs in ethanol have no significant shift compared to those in water. Comparing the PL intensities of BPQDs in both solvents, the emission intensities are stable and show only slight variation in the whole excitation region. PEG@BPQDs have also been transferred to DMSO and show the same trends like that in ethanol (Fig. S6). Therefore, the PL properties of PEG@BPQDs are stable in both the aqueous phase and organic phase, regardless of the influence of solvent polarity.

In general, the excitation-dependence of PL behavior is relevant to the particle sizes and the distribution of emission sites for most 2D dots (carbon dots, MoS2 dots, BPQDs, etc.) [36]. Theoretical calculations based on state-of-the-art time-dependent density functional theory (DFT) revealed the size and distribution dependent of the PL behavior for BPQDs [37]. The emission peak of BPQDs with smaller sizes (0.8–1.8 nm) shifts blue with size increasing, which is attributed to the amplification of the excited-state structural relaxation. For large BPQDs from 1.8 to 3.8 nm, the structure becomes rigid enough and the quantum confinement effect plays the dominant role, resulting in the red-shifted emission peaks. According to the theoretical results, the PL behavior of PEG@BPQDs is caused by the polydispersity and different emission sites on the surface of PEG@BPQDs after the passivation of NH2-PEG-NH2.

The brightness of the PL can be reflected by the absolute quantum yield. Under the excitation of 370 nm, the absolute quantum yield of PEG@BPQDs in water is 11.5%, which is greater than most BPQDs prepared in water. It is well-known that the organic passivation agent (NH2-PEG-NH2) displays the non-emission characteristics at visible wavelength due to the lack of visible chromophores [38]. So the bright luminescence emission is derived from the surface-passivated PEG@BPQDs. For comparison, the preparation details and fluorescence properties of some representative BPQDs are summarized in Table S1. The time-resolved PL decay spectrum acquired from PEG@BPQDs in water is presented in Fig. 4 (a). The emission decay line can be fitted by a biexponential function, Y(t) = A 1exp(–t/τ 1) + A 2exp(–t/τ 2), where the fractional contributions A 1 and A 2 correspond to the time-resolved decay lifetime τ 1 and τ 2, respectively. The fast decay time and the slow decay time are calculated to be 1.4 ns and 6.76 ns, respectively. The biexponential behavior of PEG@BPQDs indicates that the bright PL may originate from the trap states rather than band-edge states, which is also proved by their excitation-dependent PL behavior [16,39]. Using the equation of τ average = A 1 τ 1 2 + A 2 τ 2 2 / (A 1 τ 1 + A 2 τ 2), the average fluorescence lifetime (τ average) of PEG@BPQDs is calculated to be 5.5 ns. The short PL lifetime indicates that the emission is due to the radiative recombination of the excitons [40].

According to the reported luminescence mechanism of carbon dots, the high fluorescence from passivated PEG@BPQDs may be attributed to the presence of energy traps on the surface of PEG@BPQDs [38]. Considering the biexponential behavior of PEG@BPQDs, there are two different emissive sites derived from the BP center and surface traps in the particles [37]. Moreover, the solvothermal synthesis of P-BPQDs in ethanol shows intense and stable PL, and the time-dependent DFT demonstrates that the PL intensity is related to the formation of P–OH and P–O–CH2CH3 bonds [16]. Therefore, the P–N surface group introduced by passivation has a great influence on the electron transition channel which enhances the emission oscillator strength and PL of the PEG@BPQDs.

The photostability is particularly necessary for all practical applications of QDs. To demonstrate the photostability, we studied the effect of pH value, storage time in the atmosphere, and different concentrations of NaCl solution on the fluorescence intensity of PEG@BPQDs. Fig. 5 (a) presents the fluorescent intensity of PEG@BPQDs in the atmosphere within 40 days. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of PEG@BPQDs is stable during storage in water, indicating good PL stability. Actually, the BPQDs can keep stable PL for more than six months in water (Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity is almost invariable even in a relatively high NaCl concentration solution of 2 M, showing an excellent salt resistance. In Fig. 5(c), PEG@BPQDs also show good photostability through acid to alkaline environment, which is mainly related to the surface state because of protonation-deprotonation. The pH resistance for PEG@BPQDs ensures their applications in a wide range of pH environments. Such remarkable photostability guarantees the applications of PEG@BPQDs under variable occasions with long-term validity.

To serve as an ionic detection probe, the PL spectra of PEG@BPQDs were tested in various inorganic salt solutions (Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Bi2+, NH4 +, Co2+, Al3+, K+, Mn2+, Pb2+, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Br− and Cl−). As illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), the PL of PEG@BPQDs could be dramatically quenched only in Fe3+ solution, indicating a high selectivity of Fe3+. Moreover, as plotted in Fig. 6(b), the PL intensity of PEG@BPQDs is very sensitive to the concentration of Fe3+. The PL intensity decreases gradually with the concentration of Fe3+ increasing from 10 to 400 μM with no additional emission peaks or spectral shifts. As shown in the inset of Fig. 6(b), the fluorescence intensity difference F–F 0 is plotted vs the concentration of Fe3+. The linear calibration plot shows good linearity (R2 = 0.995) in the Fe3+ concentration range of 10–400 μM. The theoretical detection limit is calculated to be 4.7 μM by 3σ/slope, where the relative standard (σ) is 1% [41]. Moreover, we compare the performance of PEG@BPQDs with some reported probes in the detection of Fe3+, as shown in Table S2. PL decay spectrum of the mixture of PEG@BPQDs and Fe3+ was recorded to investigate the quenching mechanism of PEG@BPQDs. The lifetime in the presence of Fe3+ is calculated to be 5.7 ns from Fig. 4(b), which is similar to that of the PEG@BPQDs. The negligible change of fluorescence lifetime indicates that the fluorescence quenching is a static quenching mechanism [42]. According to the high selectivity and linear response to Fe3+, PEG@BPQDs can be possibly applied as a kind of fluorescent probe in the Fe3+ detection of industrial waste materials and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and in vitro cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Since fluoresce imaging is an important character of biomedical materials, the cytotoxicity of PEG@BPQDs should be evaluated before biomedical applications. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the viability of A549 cell is greater than 92.4% within the PEG@BPQDs concentrations of 500 μg/mL. The results indicate that PEG@BPQDs have negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility since the common concentration for bioimaging agents is usually less than 200 μg/mL. The marginal cytotoxicity could be attributed to the low toxicity of phosphorus and functionalization of NH2-PEG-NH2.

Due to the good stability, excellent biocompatibility, and PL performance, PEG@BPQDs have great potential to be applied in living cell imaging. As depicted in Fig. 7(b–d), PEG@BPQDs can be endocytosed into cells. Consequently, the cells show obvious blue PL under the excitation of 405 nm. According to the cell imaging results, PEG@BPQDs can enter the cells by caveolae-mediated endocytosis serving as cellular label PL probes [43]. Combing their low toxicity, PEG@BPQDs are a kind of promising in vitro cell imaging biomaterial.

In summary, we have developed a facile one-step synthesis method for the preparation of PEG@BPQDs directly in water via sonication-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation of bulk BP powders and the solvothermal reaction of exfoliated few-layered BP nanosheets, in which NH2-PEG-NH2 is used as the surface passivation and modification agent. The PEG@BPQDs show a high absolute quantum yield of 11.5% in water. Moreover, the fluorescence property is stable for more than 6 months without significant degradation or oxidation. At the same time, PEG@BPQDs also exhibit good resistance to acid-base and high salt environments. The PEG@BPQDs have high sensitivity and selectivity to Fe3+, which make fluorescence probes for Fe3+ in a large concentration range of 10–400 μM. Finally, PEG@BPQDs are demonstrated to have excellent biocompatibility and are a promising candidate for fluorescence imaging agents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFB2005801), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51671019, 52061135205, 51971024, 51731003, 51971023, 51927802), and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation Key Program (No. Z190007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2021.11.039.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, X.Z. Yang, W. Shao, S.C. Chen, J.F. Xie, X.D. Zhang, J. Wang, Y. Xie, J. Am. Chem. Soc.137 (2015) 11376–11382.
